Page 1

Bearing 315
The Newsletter of the Cascade Orienteering Club
July-August, 1990

KAMLOOPS - INNISFAIL - CLE ELUM

WORLD CUP ORIENTEERING

As of July 1, there are more than 700 entries for PNWOF, which will make this the biggest meet ever in the West; if we get a few hundred late and Direct Class (Wayfarer) entries, PNWOF can possibly be the largest orienteering meet ever in the USA.

Registration in competitive classes is now on a space available basis. However, even if you’ve missed the cutoff date, and for all you folks who are in orienteering because you want to have a pleasant hike in the woods with your family or friends, the Direct-classes are for you. 3 courses will be offered each day, roughly 2, 4, and 6 kilometers, quite similar to a local meet. The start and finish for these courses will be close together, and away from the competitive start/finish, and you can choose not to have your time placed with official results. The big difference from a local meet will be all the extra people, most of whom will be from a foreign country. This will be better than the Goodwill Games because no one buys a ticket to be a spectator and everyone participates (except maybe a few local townspeople). So, don't miss the most exciting orienteering event of the century.

ASIA-PACIFIC 7-DAY EVENT SCHEDULE

  • Fri, AUG 3: Model (training) Event, Kamloops
  • Sat-Sun, AUG 4-5: Canadian Orienteering Championships, Kamloops, B.C. (Days 1, 2)
  • Mon, AUG 6: APOC/Canadian Relay Championships, Kamloops, B.C.
  • Wed, AUG 8: Asia-Pacific Orienteering Chapionships, Kamloops, B.C. (Day 3)
  • Sat, AUG 11: North American Orienteering Championships & World Cup Event, Innisfail, Alberta (Day 4)
  • Sun, AUG 12: North American Orienteering Championships, Innisfail, Alberta (Day 5)
  • Tue, Wed, AUG 14-15: ROGAINE, Innisfail, Alberta
  • Fri, AUG 17: Model (training) Event, Cle Elum
  • Sat, Aug 18: Pacific Northwest O'Festival and World Cup Event, Cle Elum, Washington (Day 6)
  • Sun, Aug 19: Pacific Northwest O'Festival, Cle Elum, Washington (Day 7)

Pacific Northwest O'Festival at Hand

AND SAY FRIENDS, CAN YOU LEND A HAND?
OR A TABLE ? CHAIR? ...

Our really big Orienteering event of the decade is fast approaching -- the World Cup and Pacific Northwest O'Festival on August 18-19. And we need extra equipment to handle the large number of competitors. Can you lend any of these items to us for the Finish Area for this weekend:

  • Folding tables
  • Folding chairs
  • Battery operated clock with a large face and second hand
  • Power generator
  • Computer printer (parallel)
  • Carpenter's nail aprons

Call Jean Davis at 781-9562 if you have any of these items to lend.
Thanks!

Would You Like to be Part of a Winning Team?

The Finish Area team at the World Cup and Pacific Northwest O'Festival is going to have a starring role at this event. Be where the action is and have fun! Your choice of the following positions:

--At the wire, timing runners as they cross.
--Along the chute, collecting maps and control cards ... a good spot if you like to socialize. Here's a chance to chat with competitors from all over the world.
--In the punch checking area, for those who love shade and a cushy desk job!
--Posting results -- you'll be a much sought after person by all competitors.

(All volunteers will also have time to compete in PNWOF if they wish.)

To save yourself a spot on the Finish Area Crew call Jean Davis at 781-9562.

Page 2

From the President's Desk ... July 9, 1990

World Class orienteering! While most folks in the Seattle area are focusing on the Goodwill Games, orienteers throughout North America are looking ahead to the Asia-Pacific Orienteering Carnival: two weeks of top notch orienteering in British Columbia, Alberta, and Washington, including seven days of competition and numerous other fun activities. With several hundred participants, this will be the largest orienteering event ever in the Northwest. There is still time to be involved, either as a participant or as a volunteer (see page 1).

WSOA, the Washington State Orienteering Association, is also preparing a bid to host the 1992 US Orienteering Federation Annual Convention. Associated with the convention would be a Class A meet, similar to last year's Western States Orienteering Championships and this year's PNWOF.

Our sport continues to grow and develop. Recently, the State's sixth orienteering club was formed (Columbia River OC, Vancouver) and several maps have been newly made, revised, or reprinted in color. Meet attendance is increasing (albeit slowly) and public inquiries (from the orienteering hotline operated by COC and from other sources) are also up.

This growth and our current and future plans (PNWOF and 1992 Convention) will require more effort from our volunteers. Nearly ALL of what we accomplish as a club is dependent upon the energy of club volunteers, and the enjoyment that we get from orienteering increases in proportion to the quantity (and quality) of their contributed labors. With increased involvement by club members, orienteering (and COC in particular) will grow even greater and more enjoyable.

Yes, I am making a plea for more volunteers, a continual need in an outfit such as ours. Club leadership positions have been restructured in an attempt to make those "jobs" less overwhelming, and more manageable. Further restructuring is planned, perhaps to include amending the club's Bylaws or the creation of a committee system (that might sound terrible, but committees really can work). The overiding goal is to increase the percentage of club members involved in the club's activities. As more club members contribute to the club's operations, COC will become a correspondingly more fun organization.

There are many ways to contribute: not every volunteer needs to be an expert map maker or a hotshot course designer. The club has need of many other skills: equipment manufacture and maintenance, bookkeeping, publicity and advertising, instruction, childcare, meet staffing (registration, timing, welcoming), newsletter editing - the list goes on. As an example, a year or so ago, a control marker "sewing party" was held. With a half dozen sewing machines and various other pieces of equipment, we made two dozen markers in one enjoyable afternoon (several of those helping out did not have any sewing experience, but nonetheless were able to contribute). We have control markers at our events, thanks to efforts such as this.

Please contribute. To volunteer for meet staffing, please call Ruth McCormick (324-8057); to design or set courses, or to direct an event, please call David Soper and Denise Klein (322-7797); and for other "administrative" positions, please call me (783-3960).
Thank You!

Mike Schuh, President

Club Meeting July 19

Our next club meeting will be on Thursday, July 19 at 7:00 p.m. at the University Branch Library (50th and Roosevelt) in Seattle. Mike Schuh will present a slide shop of his recent orienteering trip to the Soviet Union. Everyone is welcome, so come and see how they do orienteering in the Soviet Union.

Newsletter Editor Still Needed

After four years, Debbie Newell has retired as editor of Bearing 315, although she will still collect and tabulate meet results. I have agreed to help with the newsletter through the rest of this year, but the job needs an editor So, somebody out there, here's your chance to be a newsletter editor.
If you think the newsletter is late and a little ragged this time, any volunteers will be accepted to speed things up next issue.
Bruce McAlister, interim editor

Pic - "O" -Nic

Our August Pot-luck will be a pot-luck picnic, hosted by Don Atkinson and Bruce McAlister on Saturday August 25. Come, relax, and exchange all your PNWOF tales. The picnic is at the Atkinson and Robert McAlister homes at Arrowhead Point. To reach the picnic site, take Juanita Drive as if you were going to St Edwards, but take the next road north of the St Edwards road to where it ends at the lake. The Arrowhead School will be on your left as you enter Arrowhead Drive. The school will be the start of a fun run in St Edwards, ending at the picnic site (we encourage you to park at the school, parking is limited at the lake). The course will open at 1 p.m., the picnic will start at 2 p.m., and last until ??. There will be food, fun, swimming, sailing, and for the more adventurous, water skis and sail boards. Bring the family and join us for a pleasant afternoon of relaxing after the stress of seven A meets in 16 days. A special invitation is extended to Sammamish and Nisqually and WIOL orienteers. Pot-O-Luck coordinator is Eileen Breseman (522-1098).

Page 5

Cascade Chatter

compiled by Harvey Friedman

As usual, we'll start with positive roster changes as of 30 June 1990. Words of both thanks and welcome go to the following new or renewing club members: Mia Barbera, Peter Blomquist, Peter Golde, Jessica Rykken, Rob Simpson, Gail Smith & Craig Wyss, David Soper & Denise Klein, Tim Vargo family, Sharon Wilson. If you all don't come to meets, you'll probably not see your name in the newsletter until the next time you renew; also, membership cards are only available in person at a club function such as regular meet, meeting, pot-O-luck, but not at any ski-O meets, nor night-O meets, nor canoe-O meets (unless you are lucky), nor training events, nor workshops. If you want to get your handsome membership card, contact the membership coordinator, me (Harvey), at a Cascade sponsored meet or at one of the above-mentioned happenings. I do not have blank membership cards with me when I attend other clubs' functions. If you move, don't forget to turn in a change of address card; you don't want your newsletter to be late (or lost).

Now on to the fun stuff...Some of you might have seen former club member, Ingemar Jansson, at the meet at Moran State Park in June. Despite not being in very good condition (for him) because there was not enough snow in southern Norway for cross-country skiing last winter, Ingemar took 1st place both days on the hardest course. He also provided a valuable service to the efforts of the World Cup and PNWOF90 organizers. The cartography and printing of the maps to be used for the Model Event and for Saturday 18 August was done in Norway. The cartographer, Kristen Treekrem, one of the best, lives in Oslo and was doing a map for Ingemar's club when we talked. So, Ingemar carried about 130 pounds of maps with him when he came to visit - he related funny stories about taxi drivers' responses to such heavy pieces of baggage. Unfortunately, he will not be one of the 600 plus participating in our August meet....Goran & Berit Larsson and their children do plan to attend PNWOF, along with her parents. Hard to believe he now runs in 40A....Speaking of Europe, club president Mike Schuh spent a week in the Soviet Union in May as part of the Gary McCausland organized exchange visit with the O' club in Olympia's sister city Pskov, which is in Russia. In exchange, about a half dozen folks from Pskov will participate in PNWOF and probably the other APOC90 7-day events as well.... Jan Vrana will have become a "Yankee" by the time you read this; the ceremony was scheduled for July 4th, naturally. Jan has decided to change from tile setter to tile artist; his mosaic will be part of Goodwill Games and will be in Westlake Mall after they are over....If you want more news about people in the club, you'll have to tell me something or preferably write or scribble me something that is for this column since I have such a bad memory.

Page 6

the Washington State Orienteering Association presents

1990 Pacific Northwest Orienteering Festival

August 17-19

Cle Elum, Washington

This application form is for those orienteers who will enter PNWOF only. If you wish to enter any of the Canadian orienteering days of the Asia-Pacific Orienteering Championships 90 event or be eligible for an APOC90 Seven Day Award, you must use the regular APOC90 application form. These forms are available from Harvey Friedman at any Cascade Orienteering Club meet.

Join in the world class atmosphere at the Pacific Northwest Orienteering Festival. This year, Day 1 of PNWOF will be held in conjunction with a World Cup event. Prequalified World Cup teams will compete on the same map that the PNWOF courses will be run on; hence, we need to be vague about the exact location of the competition area. PNWOF will be held in Kittitas County, Washington about 150 km (90 mi) east of Seattle or about 320km (200 mi) west of Spokane by I-90. Participants arrange their own accommodations.

Meet Director - Harvey Friedman (206)784-2774. Registrars - Robert Miller and Sandy Kish Miller (206)784-5939 days. Course Designers - Bob & Mary Forbes, Fred Veler, and Anne York.

Maps - 1:15,000, IOF 5 color, 5-meter contour interval. Day 1: new map; base map by ProOrienteering; fieldchecked 1989 by Åre Kristiansen & Vidar Strand. Day 2: 1990 re-fieldchecked and redrawn version of 1981 Cle Elum Ridge map.

Terrain - the forest is predominantly ponderosa pine of variable runnability. Day 1: abandoned coal mining operations add an interesting complexity to the moderately steep terrain which ranges in elevation from 500 to 1090 meters.

Competition Center - Thursday - the lobby of Courson Hall at Central Washington University, 8th St. & Walnut in Ellensburg; Friday-Sunday - Cle Elum High School, Rte. 4, Cle Elum. Check the schedule below for the hours open.

Competitive Classes - please see the attached entry form for classes offered. Day-of-meet registration is available only for wayfarer's courses (see below). You must preregister for competitive classes.

Awards for the top three finishers in each competitive class. Classes may be combined if there are insufficient entries.

Wayfarer Courses - each day there will be three wayfarer courses: beginner, intermediate and advanced. For wayfarer courses, registration is on the day of the event; there is no pregistration. Instruction and limited compass rental will be available. The registration fee for wayfarer courses will be $5.00 per map, per day.

A String-O will be available each day. The registration fee for the string-O will be $1.00 per map, per day.

A Model Event will be offered on Friday, August 17 for anyone, cost $4.00. A model event is a non-competitive event designed to simulate the conditions that will be present during the actual meet. The map for the model event was produced by the same people who produced the Day 1 competition map. You may do any of the available courses, but no times will be recorded.

Child Care is available (only while competing, please) at $1 per child, per hour. Please indicate on the registration form if needed.

Whistles are required of all entrants, on all courses and will be checked at Start Areas. They will be on sale at the event center.

T-shirts (100% cotton w/short sleeves) celebrating both the World Cup and PNWOF can be ordered on the registration form.

Saturday Night Social - there will be a Social Event at the Central Washington University Student Union Ballroom on Saturday, August 18 at 8 p.m. Although details are still to be worked out, we may have a dance. The expected cost, payable at the door, will be approximately $5.00 per person, with a discount for those staying at CWU. Come join the fun!

SCHEDULE

  • Friday, June 1: Last day for early bird registrations to be received.
  • Saturday, July 21: Last day preregistrations for PNWOF to be accepted.
  • Thursday, August 16: 1700-2200 Registration packet pickup at CWU.
  • Friday, August 17:
    • 0900-1200 Model Event Registration and Registration packet pickup at Cle Elum High School
    • 1000-1400 Model Event (for training purposes)
    • 1600 World Cup Start Draw
    • 1700-2200 Registration packet pickup at Cle Elum High School
  • Saturday, August 18:
    • 0700-1100 Wayfarer Registration and Registration packet pickup at Cle Elum High School
    • 0900 First Start PNWOF
    • 0900 First Start World Cup Event
    • 2000-2200 Social Event in Ellensburg
  • Sunday, August 19:
    • 0700-1100 Wayfarer Registration and Registration packet pickup at Cle Elum High School
    • 0900 First Start PNWOF
    • 1500 Awards Presentation

Please allow 45 minutes to get from Cle Elum High School to the meet start area.

block_index.text: Bearing 315 The Newsletter of the Cascade Orienteering Club July-August, 1990

As of July 1, there are more than 700 entries for PNWOF, which will make this the biggest meet ever in the West; if we get a few hundred late and Direct Class (Wayfarer) entries, PNWOF can possibly be the largest orienteering meet ever in the USA.

Registration in competitive classes is now on a space available basis. However, even if you’ve missed the cutoff date, and for all you folks who are in orienteering because you want to have a pleasant hike in the woods with your family or friends, the Direct-classes are for you. 3 courses will be offered each day, roughly 2, 4, and 6 kilometers, quite similar to a local meet. The start and finish for these courses will be close together, and away from the competitive start/finish, and you can choose not to have your time placed with official results. The big difference from a local meet will be all the extra people, most of whom will be from a foreign country. This will be better than the Goodwill Games because no one buys a ticket to be a spectator and everyone participates (except maybe a few local townspeople). So, don't miss the most exciting orienteering event of the century.
Sat-Sun, AUG 4-5: Canadian Orienteering Championships, Kamloops, B.C. (Days 1, 2)
Mon, AUG 6: APOC/Canadian Relay Championships, Kamloops, B.C.
Wed, AUG 8: Asia-Pacific Orienteering Chapionships, Kamloops, B.C. (Day 3)
Sat, AUG 11: North American Orienteering Championships & World Cup Event, Innisfail, Alberta (Day 4)
Sun, AUG 12: North American Orienteering Championships, Innisfail, Alberta (Day 5)
Tue, Wed, AUG 14-15: ROGAINE, Innisfail, Alberta
Fri, AUG 17: Model (training) Event, Cle Elum
Sat, Aug 18: Pacific Northwest O'Festival and World Cup Event, Cle Elum, Washington (Day 6)
Sun, Aug 19: Pacific Northwest O'Festival, Cle Elum, Washington (Day 7)
AND SAY FRIENDS, CAN YOU LEND A HAND? OR A TABLE ? CHAIR? ...

Our really big Orienteering event of the decade is fast approaching -- the World Cup and Pacific Northwest O'Festival on August 18-19. And we need extra equipment to handle the large number of competitors. Can you lend any of these items to us for the Finish Area for this weekend:
Battery operated clock with a large face and second hand
Call Jean Davis at 781-9562 if you have any of these items to lend. Thanks!
Would You Like to be Part of a Winning Team?
The Finish Area team at the World Cup and Pacific Northwest O'Festival is going to have a starring role at this event. Be where the action is and have fun! Your choice of the following positions:

--At the wire, timing runners as they cross. --Along the chute, collecting maps and control cards ... a good spot if you like to socialize. Here's a chance to chat with competitors from all over the world. --In the punch checking area, for those who love shade and a cushy desk job! --Posting results -- you'll be a much sought after person by all competitors.
(All volunteers will also have time to compete in PNWOF if they wish.)
To save yourself a spot on the Finish Area Crew call Jean Davis at 781-9562.

World Class orienteering! While most folks in the Seattle area are focusing on the Goodwill Games, orienteers throughout North America are looking ahead to the Asia-Pacific Orienteering Carnival: two weeks of top notch orienteering in British Columbia, Alberta, and Washington, including seven days of competition and numerous other fun activities. With several hundred participants, this will be the largest orienteering event ever in the Northwest. There is still time to be involved, either as a participant or as a volunteer (see page 1).

WSOA, the Washington State Orienteering Association, is also preparing a bid to host the 1992 US Orienteering Federation Annual Convention. Associated with the convention would be a Class A meet, similar to last year's Western States Orienteering Championships and this year's PNWOF.

Our sport continues to grow and develop. Recently, the State's sixth orienteering club was formed (Columbia River OC, Vancouver) and several maps have been newly made, revised, or reprinted in color. Meet attendance is increasing (albeit slowly) and public inquiries (from the orienteering hotline operated by COC and from other sources) are also up.

This growth and our current and future plans (PNWOF and 1992 Convention) will require more effort from our volunteers. Nearly ALL of what we accomplish as a club is dependent upon the energy of club volunteers, and the enjoyment that we get from orienteering increases in proportion to the quantity (and quality) of their contributed labors. With increased involvement by club members, orienteering (and COC in particular) will grow even greater and more enjoyable.

Yes, I am making a plea for more volunteers, a continual need in an outfit such as ours. Club leadership positions have been restructured in an attempt to make those "jobs" less overwhelming, and more manageable. Further restructuring is planned, perhaps to include amending the club's Bylaws or the creation of a committee system (that might sound terrible, but committees really can work). The overiding goal is to increase the percentage of club members involved in the club's activities. As more club members contribute to the club's operations, COC will become a correspondingly more fun organization.

There are many ways to contribute: not every volunteer needs to be an expert map maker or a hotshot course designer. The club has need of many other skills: equipment manufacture and maintenance, bookkeeping, publicity and advertising, instruction, childcare, meet staffing (registration, timing, welcoming), newsletter editing - the list goes on. As an example, a year or so ago, a control marker "sewing party" was held. With a half dozen sewing machines and various other pieces of equipment, we made two dozen markers in one enjoyable afternoon (several of those helping out did not have any sewing experience, but nonetheless were able to contribute). We have control markers at our events, thanks to efforts such as this.

Please contribute. To volunteer for meet staffing, please call Ruth McCormick (324-8057); to design or set courses, or to direct an event, please call David Soper and Denise Klein (322-7797); and for other "administrative" positions, please call me (783-3960). Thank You!

Our next club meeting will be on Thursday, July 19 at 7:00 p.m. at the University Branch Library (50th and Roosevelt) in Seattle. Mike Schuh will present a slide shop of his recent orienteering trip to the Soviet Union. Everyone is welcome, so come and see how they do orienteering in the Soviet Union.

After four years, Debbie Newell has retired as editor of Bearing 315, although she will still collect and tabulate meet results. I have agreed to help with the newsletter through the rest of this year, but the job needs an editor So, somebody out there, here's your chance to be a newsletter editor. If you think the newsletter is late and a little ragged this time, any volunteers will be accepted to speed things up next issue. Bruce McAlister, interim editor

Our August Pot-luck will be a pot-luck picnic, hosted by Don Atkinson and Bruce McAlister on Saturday August 25. Come, relax, and exchange all your PNWOF tales. The picnic is at the Atkinson and Robert McAlister homes at Arrowhead Point. To reach the picnic site, take Juanita Drive as if you were going to St Edwards, but take the next road north of the St Edwards road to where it ends at the lake. The Arrowhead School will be on your left as you enter Arrowhead Drive. The school will be the start of a fun run in St Edwards, ending at the picnic site (we encourage you to park at the school, parking is limited at the lake). The course will open at 1 p.m., the picnic will start at 2 p.m., and last until ??. There will be food, fun, swimming, sailing, and for the more adventurous, water skis and sail boards. Bring the family and join us for a pleasant afternoon of relaxing after the stress of seven A meets in 16 days. A special invitation is extended to Sammamish and Nisqually and WIOL orienteers. Pot-O-Luck coordinator is Eileen Breseman (522-1098).
LUTHER BURBANK PARK - May 9 Sammamish Orienteering Club

Peanuts [Comic strip featuring Snoopy and Woodstock in scouting gear] Panel 1: "SO HERE I AM AGAIN RIDING ON THE BACK OF MOM'S BICYCLE.." Panel 2: "MOM DOES THE PEDALING AND THE STEERING, AND I DO THE NAVIGATING..." Panel 3: "KEEP THE SUN ON OUR LEFT, THE OCEAN ON OUR RIGHT, THE RIVER ON OUR LEFT AND THE NORTH STAR IN FRONT.." Panel 4: "LOST AGAIN!"

As usual, we'll start with positive roster changes as of 30 June 1990. Words of both thanks and welcome go to the following new or renewing club members: Mia Barbera, Peter Blomquist, Peter Golde, Jessica Rykken, Rob Simpson, Gail Smith & Craig Wyss, David Soper & Denise Klein, Tim Vargo family, Sharon Wilson. If you all don't come to meets, you'll probably not see your name in the newsletter until the next time you renew; also, membership cards are only available in person at a club function such as regular meet, meeting, pot-O-luck, but not at any ski-O meets, nor night-O meets, nor canoe-O meets (unless you are lucky), nor training events, nor workshops. If you want to get your handsome membership card, contact the membership coordinator, me (Harvey), at a Cascade sponsored meet or at one of the above-mentioned happenings. I do not have blank membership cards with me when I attend other clubs' functions. If you move, don't forget to turn in a change of address card; you don't want your newsletter to be late (or lost).

Now on to the fun stuff...Some of you might have seen former club member, Ingemar Jansson, at the meet at Moran State Park in June. Despite not being in very good condition (for him) because there was not enough snow in southern Norway for cross-country skiing last winter, Ingemar took 1st place both days on the hardest course. He also provided a valuable service to the efforts of the World Cup and PNWOF90 organizers. The cartography and printing of the maps to be used for the Model Event and for Saturday 18 August was done in Norway. The cartographer, Kristen Treekrem, one of the best, lives in Oslo and was doing a map for Ingemar's club when we talked. So, Ingemar carried about 130 pounds of maps with him when he came to visit - he related funny stories about taxi drivers' responses to such heavy pieces of baggage. Unfortunately, he will not be one of the 600 plus participating in our August meet....Goran & Berit Larsson and their children do plan to attend PNWOF, along with her parents. Hard to believe he now runs in 40A....Speaking of Europe, club president Mike Schuh spent a week in the Soviet Union in May as part of the Gary McCausland organized exchange visit with the O' club in Olympia's sister city Pskov, which is in Russia. In exchange, about a half dozen folks from Pskov will participate in PNWOF and probably the other APOC90 7-day events as well.... Jan Vrana will have become a "Yankee" by the time you read this; the ceremony was scheduled for July 4th, naturally. Jan has decided to change from tile setter to tile artist; his mosaic will be part of Goodwill Games and will be in Westlake Mall after they are over....If you want more news about people in the club, you'll have to tell me something or preferably write or scribble me something that is for this column since I have such a bad memory.
Families (regular fee for first 2 family members, $1.00/person thereafter)
Special events, such as the State Championships and "A" meets, have higher fees

"BEARING 315" PUBLICATION SCHEDULE "Bearing 315" is a bi-monthly publication of the Cascade Orienteering Club. You are encouraged to submit orienteering related material for publication. Send items for publication to the address below by the scheduled deadline. Material received after the deadline may not appear in the next newsletter issue.
Mail material to: Bruce McAlister 14014 81st Pl. NE Bothell, WA 98011
the Washington State Orienteering Association presents

This application form is for those orienteers who will enter PNWOF only. If you wish to enter any of the Canadian orienteering days of the Asia-Pacific Orienteering Championships 90 event or be eligible for an APOC90 Seven Day Award, you must use the regular APOC90 application form. These forms are available from Harvey Friedman at any Cascade Orienteering Club meet.

Join in the world class atmosphere at the Pacific Northwest Orienteering Festival. This year, Day 1 of PNWOF will be held in conjunction with a World Cup event. Prequalified World Cup teams will compete on the same map that the PNWOF courses will be run on; hence, we need to be vague about the exact location of the competition area. PNWOF will be held in Kittitas County, Washington about 150 km (90 mi) east of Seattle or about 320km (200 mi) west of Spokane by I-90. Participants arrange their own accommodations.
Meet Director - Harvey Friedman (206)784-2774. Registrars - Robert Miller and Sandy Kish Miller (206)784-5939 days. Course Designers - Bob & Mary Forbes, Fred Veler, and Anne York.

Maps - 1:15,000, IOF 5 color, 5-meter contour interval. Day 1: new map; base map by ProOrienteering; fieldchecked 1989 by Åre Kristiansen & Vidar Strand. Day 2: 1990 re-fieldchecked and redrawn version of 1981 Cle Elum Ridge map.

Terrain - the forest is predominantly ponderosa pine of variable runnability. Day 1: abandoned coal mining operations add an interesting complexity to the moderately steep terrain which ranges in elevation from 500 to 1090 meters.

Competition Center - Thursday - the lobby of Courson Hall at Central Washington University, 8th St. & Walnut in Ellensburg; Friday-Sunday - Cle Elum High School, Rte. 4, Cle Elum. Check the schedule below for the hours open.

Competitive Classes - please see the attached entry form for classes offered. Day-of-meet registration is available only for wayfarer's courses (see below). You must preregister for competitive classes.
Awards for the top three finishers in each competitive class. Classes may be combined if there are insufficient entries.

Wayfarer Courses - each day there will be three wayfarer courses: beginner, intermediate and advanced. For wayfarer courses, registration is on the day of the event; there is no pregistration. Instruction and limited compass rental will be available. The registration fee for wayfarer courses will be $5.00 per map, per day.
A String-O will be available each day. The registration fee for the string-O will be $1.00 per map, per day.

A Model Event will be offered on Friday, August 17 for anyone, cost $4.00. A model event is a non-competitive event designed to simulate the conditions that will be present during the actual meet. The map for the model event was produced by the same people who produced the Day 1 competition map. You may do any of the available courses, but no times will be recorded.
Child Care is available (only while competing, please) at $1 per child, per hour. Please indicate on the registration form if needed.
Whistles are required of all entrants, on all courses and will be checked at Start Areas. They will be on sale at the event center.
T-shirts (100% cotton w/short sleeves) celebrating both the World Cup and PNWOF can be ordered on the registration form.

Saturday Night Social - there will be a Social Event at the Central Washington University Student Union Ballroom on Saturday, August 18 at 8 p.m. Although details are still to be worked out, we may have a dance. The expected cost, payable at the door, will be approximately $5.00 per person, with a discount for those staying at CWU. Come join the fun!
Friday, June 1: Last day for early bird registrations to be received.
Saturday, July 21: Last day preregistrations for PNWOF to be accepted.
Thursday, August 16: 1700-2200 Registration packet pickup at CWU.

Friday, August 17: 0900-1200 Model Event Registration and Registration packet pickup at Cle Elum High School 1000-1400 Model Event (for training purposes) 1600 World Cup Start Draw 1700-2200 Registration packet pickup at Cle Elum High School

Saturday, August 18: 0700-1100 Wayfarer Registration and Registration packet pickup at Cle Elum High School 0900 First Start PNWOF 0900 First Start World Cup Event 2000-2200 Social Event in Ellensburg
Sunday, August 19: 0700-1100 Wayfarer Registration and Registration packet pickup at Cle Elum High School 0900 First Start PNWOF 1500 Awards Presentation
Please allow 45 minutes to get from Cle Elum High School to the meet start area.
